video_title: How a Major Grocery Store Chain Can Dramatically Lower the Cost of Food | Odd Lots
gist: Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer Scott Patton explains how the discounter's SKU rationalization (~2,000 SKUs vs. 30,000+ at rivals), ~90% private-label penetration, and supplier-co-designed packaging/logistics create a durable cost and throughput advantage, letting Aldi open a profitable 25,000 sq ft Times Square store at nationwide-uniform prices while it observes consumers trading down from beef to chicken amid 70-year-high beef prices and a GLP-1-linked protein boom.

insights.claim: Aldi stocks roughly 2,000 core SKUs and deliberately limits choice within categories (e.g., 2 ketchup SKUs vs. an industry-typical 30-40, 4 olive oil options vs. 50-60 elsewhere), using narrow assortments to cut complexity and restocking labor.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: SKU rationalization is a structural, hard-to-replicate cost advantage for discount grocers versus full-assortment chains, useful for evaluating grocery retail margin structures.
insights.quote: We had two ketchup SKUs.

insights.claim: Because Aldi's private-label products are Aldi's own, the company pays no slotting fees to place items on shelves, unlike traditional retailers where suppliers pay for premium shelf space, directly supporting lower shelf prices.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Slotting fees are a hidden cost embedded in branded CPG retail economics; high private-label penetration structurally removes this cost, favoring discounters' price position.
insights.quote: With our private label we have two ketchups. There's no shelf space, there's no slotting fees.

insights.claim: Aldi's private-label penetration is roughly 90% of its shelf versus an estimated 25-30% at competitors, and the company is currently rebranding from over 90 different in-house sub-brand names down to about 23 (putting the Aldi name on package fronts) after research found customers didn't realize products like Berman's or Sweet Harvest were Aldi-owned.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A shift from fragmented private-label sub-brands to a unified branded-house architecture signals Aldi converting its cost advantage into consumer-facing brand equity, a strategic move worth tracking for competitive pressure on national brands.
insights.quote: So we're switching from 90 brands down to about 23 now.

insights.claim: Private-label products carry roughly a 30-50% cost differential versus national branded equivalents, but developing them requires far more operational investment (in-house recipe development, test kitchens, direct manufacturer negotiation) than simply buying and reselling a national brand.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: The private-label cost advantage is an operating-model moat built over decades of buyer expertise, not just a sourcing trick, making it a genuine barrier to entry against copycat store brands.
insights.quote: You're talking 30 to 40 or 50% difference.

insights.claim: Narrower assortments (4 olive oil SKUs vs. 50-60 elsewhere) increased customer purchase confidence in informal in-store observation by Aldi's buying team, who found shoppers felt less confident choosing among 60 options than among 4.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Choice-overload effects support curated-assortment retailers winning on customer experience, not just price, relevant to how discounters defend share against larger-assortment rivals.
insights.quote: The customers felt less confident in their purchase.

insights.claim: Aldi's new ~25,000 sq ft Times Square store requires 3-4 truckloads of groceries per day, delivered overnight using shortened trucks with two drivers each (one to navigate Manhattan's tight turning radii, one to unload) because standard trucks cannot maneuver the streets, a logistics cost baked directly into the store's unit economics.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Quantifies why urban/last-mile grocery delivery costs are structurally higher, a factor for modeling urban grocery real estate or quick-commerce unit economics.
insights.quote: It's a bit of a shorter truck because they can't hit the turning radius... we actually have to have two drivers.

insights.claim: Aldi maintains uniform national pricing (identical shelf prices in Manhattan and suburban Connecticut) despite materially higher Manhattan rent and delivery costs, absorbing those extra urban costs into overall network economics rather than passing them through via local pricing.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Runs counter to typical geographic price discrimination in retail; suggests Aldi is using network-wide scale to subsidize urban expansion as a volume and brand play, which could pressure competitors' urban pricing power.
insights.quote: Your store would have the same prices, the same price in New York, as out in Connecticut.

insights.claim: Aldi contracts directly with produce growers (e.g., grape growers in California, Mexico, and Chile) 3-4 years in advance of harvest, dedicating specific grower plots to Aldi's chosen varieties and volumes, with product moving from grower to Aldi distribution centers to stores often the next day, bypassing traditional wholesale/broker layers.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Multi-year vertical integration into grower relationships (versus spot-buying from local produce markets) is a strategic lever for cost and freshness control, relevant to assessing any grocer's supply-chain resilience.
insights.quote: It really starts probably 3 to 4 years in advance.

insights.claim: Aldi is deliberately opening large-format stores in ultra-high-rent locations like two blocks from Times Square and expects the location to be genuinely profitable on grocery sales alone rather than merely a brand billboard, citing heavy tourist/commuter foot traffic and e-commerce fulfillment potential.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Indicates discount grocers see profitable unit economics in prime urban real estate previously dominated by premium/flexible-format grocers, a trend to watch for further discount-grocer urban infill.
insights.quote: We intend to be very profitable at the store... That would be a very expensive billboard if we weren't going to sell a lot of groceries.

insights.claim: Aldi now operates 14 stores in the New York metro area, having entered via a Harlem location, with a second Harlem store opening next year, indicating an accelerating densification strategy in NYC beyond the Times Square flagship.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Concrete data point on discount-grocer expansion in a historically underserved low-cost-grocery urban market, relevant to competitive dynamics for Whole Foods, Trader Joe's, and other NYC grocers.

insights.claim: Switching from physical price tags to digital electronic shelf labels saved Aldi roughly 3-4 hours of labor per store per week across its ~2,600 US locations, savings redirected into lower shelf prices; Aldi states it does not and will not use the labels for dynamic/surge pricing.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A concrete labor-savings figure (3-4 hrs/store/week across ~2,600 stores) for modeling retail labor productivity gains from electronic shelf label rollouts industry-wide.
insights.quote: We were spending between 3 and 4 hours per store per week, to change out our signs.

insights.claim: Aldi requires private-label suppliers to package products in pre-designed 'display-ready' cases that staff can place directly on shelves without unpacking individual units, which it says cuts restocking time to roughly 2% of the time required to stock a comparable multi-SKU assortment at a traditional store.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Packaging-level supply chain redesign co-developed with suppliers is a rarely executed lever for reducing in-store labor cost, a differentiator to watch for as competitors try to close the gap with discounters.
insights.quote: We can literally restock all of our olive oil... probably 2% of the time that it takes to stock another store.

insights.claim: Aldi engineers multiple hidden barcodes (4-6 per package) onto its private-label products purely to speed checkout scanning, something branded CPG companies are reluctant to do because it consumes package space they prefer to use for marketing copy and brand storytelling.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A structural conflict between national brands (packaging optimized for marketing) and private-label retailers (packaging optimized for throughput) is a durable reason branded-heavy retailers can't easily match discounter checkout speed and labor costs.
insights.quote: Since we control the product and the packaging, we can put 20 barcodes if we wanted to.

insights.claim: Aldi delayed adopting barcode scanning until 1998, later than competitors, because its cashiers had memorized store prices and entered them manually via ten-key pads faster than available scanners could scan; Aldi says it helped drive development of 'bi-level' scanners specifically to catch up to its own manual-entry speed.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A striking historical example of a retailer's efficiency culture outpacing and shaping available technology, illustrating Aldi's decades-long institutional focus on throughput as its core competitive lever.
insights.quote: Our cashiers were they were too fast. We couldn't find a scanning system that was fast enough to keep up with our ten key.

insights.claim: Self-checkout remains limited at Aldi (roughly one-third of stores) and was deliberately excluded from the new Times Square flagship, because RFID tagging is still too expensive for low-value produce and self-checkout still struggles with age verification and weighing produce; Aldi's staffed checkout lanes are considered fast enough that self-checkout isn't needed to keep lines short.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Counters the industry-wide push toward self-checkout automation; a well-run staffed-checkout model built on operational efficiency can outperform self-checkout on both cost and speed, a caution against assuming self-checkout capex is a universal margin lever.
insights.quote: RFID hasn't come to grocery yet. RFID is a pretty expensive technology when you're talking about selling avocados and tomatoes.

themes.theme: Consumer Demand Shifts: Protein, GLP-1, and Trade-Down

insights.claim: Beef prices are at 70-year highs (Aldi ground beef running $5.99-$6.99/lb), and Aldi is observing consumers substitute toward ground turkey and ground chicken, which run roughly 30-40% cheaper, while still prioritizing overall protein purchases.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A granular, live read on protein-category trade-down behavior amid input-cost inflation, useful for modeling beef/poultry demand elasticity and packaged-meat margin mix shift.
insights.quote: Beef prices are at 70 year highs.

insights.claim: Aldi's supplier partners estimated roughly 5% of the US population was using GLP-1 drugs about 18 months before this conversation, and Aldi expects adoption to rise further as GLP-1s move into pill form, linking this directly to accelerating protein-focused product sales across categories.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A grocer's ground-level GLP-1 penetration estimate is a useful cross-check for modeling downstream effects on food categories and the overlap between pharma adoption and consumer-staples demand.
insights.quote: I think it was roughly 5% of the population at that time was on GLP ones.

insights.claim: The protein trend has spread beyond meat into protein bagels, protein popcorn, and protein sparkling water at Aldi, and the company expects this super-cycle to eventually pull back, with fiber-focused products (a newly launched Greek chickpea SKU, fiber water) already showing resurgence as a related, later-stage trend.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Broad category penetration signals the protein boom is maturing (a late-cycle indicator) while fiber is emerging as the next functional-nutrition trend, useful for positioning in functional-food and CPG innovation.
insights.quote: I think there'll be a pullback at some point.

insights.claim: Aldi's sourdough bread SKUs, launched a few years ago in response to the pandemic-era home-baking trend, have become the top-performing item in its bread set, with the company now selling over 500,000 loaves per week network-wide.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A quantified example of a social/pandemic-driven food trend converting into a durable, large-scale SKU win rather than fading, evidence for how grocers turn viral trends into repeatable revenue.
insights.quote: We are selling, you know, over half a million loaves of sour dough every single week in our stores.

insights.claim: Because Aldi's products are private label with direct manufacturer relationships, it can respond to viral demand spikes (e.g., a feta-and-tomato pasta recipe that went viral on social media) within a couple of months by directly negotiating more production line time and packaging changes with suppliers, a speed advantage it says branded retailers lack because they must request more supply from brand owners who serve every other retailer too.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Direct-to-manufacturer private-label relationships give discount grocers a faster demand-response supply chain than brand-dependent retailers, a structural agility advantage when social-media-driven demand shocks hit food categories.
insights.quote: If we were a brand, we'd have to call them and say, hey, can we get more of your brand that you're sending to every other retailer? We go directly to the manufacturer.

insights.claim: Aldi's decision on which national brands to carry alongside its private label (e.g., stocking Hellmann's and Sweet Baby Ray's but not Heinz ketchup) is driven by measured private-label acceptance/penetration data within each category and by 'iconic brand' loyalty signals including social media chatter, rather than gut feel.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: A data-driven category-by-category framework for how discount retailers decide brand-vs-private-label shelf allocation, useful for CPG companies assessing which categories are most defensible against private-label share loss.
insights.quote: There's two, there's I would call the data point would be private label penetration.

insights.claim: Traditional grocers' wide branded assortments are partly a function of shelf-space monetization, where suppliers who pay more secure more shelf space, which Aldi's CCO frames as a structural reason legacy retailers carry more SKUs than customers may actually need.
insights.speaker: Scott Patton, Aldi US Chief Commercial Officer
insights.so_what: Reveals a margin source for traditional grocers (slotting/shelf fees) that also functions as a cost center passed to consumers via reduced price competitiveness, relevant to assessing legacy grocers' structural cost disadvantage versus discounters.
insights.quote: It can come down to shelf space. You know, which suppliers are paying you to be on their shelf... sometimes the more you pay, the more shelf space you get.
